sentences.text: Okay, so let's get started.
So before the break, we have mentioned that you should try to train a model with two layers and a model with six layers.
And one thing that you may have observed is that a six-layer model may not be as good as a simple model.
And this is weird because we have mentioned that deep neural network becomes powerful because we have more layers.
But now this is not the case.
So in the rest of this lecture, we are going to discuss several practical aspects in training neural network models.
So let's start with the easy thing.
Let's consider a practical scenario in our real life.
That is, I may want to predict who will repay their loan and who will default.
So in such a scenario, a lender helps you to investigate this problem and you are given the files of 100 applicants.
Among these 100 applicants, there are five of them defaulted within three years.
So your goal is to build a model and predict who else is going to default.
For such a job, what you may find is they give you some photos and they find that all the five people who defaulted worn blue shirts during their interviews.
And your model also find that blue shirts is a strong signal for default.
So whether this is a valid feature or whether there are some problem when we make this conclusion.
This is a very classic problem of overfitting or there's no codal evidence that blue shirts actually predict applicants default behavior, right?
The reason is because all of them are wearing blue shirts.
So you cannot say only those who default wear such a blue shirts.
This is one thing.
And the other thing is using human logic, we know that the color of the shirts should have no codal inference with whether he's going to default or not.
So because of that, we know that this feature may not be a valid feature.
And this introduced our classic discussion of training error and generalization error.
training error means the error on the training data set.
And generalization error refers to your error on a new data.
And we always say that the performance on the training data does not represent the performance on a new data set.
This is very similar to we make practices for a...

aligned_segments.text: Okay, so let's get started.
So before the break, we have mentioned that you should try to train a model with two layers and a model with six layers.
And one thing that you may have observed is that a six-layer model may not be as good as a simple model.
And this is weird because we have mentioned that deep neural network becomes powerful because we have more layers.
But now this is not the case.
So in the rest of this lecture, we are going to discuss several practical aspects in training neural network models.
So let's start with the easy thing.
Let's consider a practical scenario in our real life.

aligned_pages.ppt_text: Predict Who Will Repay TheirLoans •A lender hires you to investigate who will repay their  loans •You are given complete files on 100 •5 defaulted within 3 years Image creditdebt.org

aligned_segments.text: That is, I may want to predict who will repay their loan and who will default.
So in such a scenario, a lender helps you to investigate this problem and you are given the files of 100 applicants.
Among these 100 applicants, there are five of them defaulted within three years.
So your goal is to build a model and predict who else is going to default.

aligned_pages.ppt_text:
A Surprising Finding
• All 5 people who defaulted wore blue shirts during interviews
• Your model may find this strong signal as well

aligned_segments.text: For such a job, what you may find is they give you some photos and they find that all the five people who defaulted worn blue shirts during their interviews.
And your model also find that blue shirts is a strong signal for default.
So whether this is a valid feature or whether there are some problem when we make this conclusion.
This is a very classic problem of overfitting or there's no codal evidence that blue shirts actually predict applicants default behavior, right?
The reason is because all of them are wearing blue shirts.
So you cannot say only those who default wear such a blue shirts.
This is one thing.
And the other thing is using human logic, we know that the color of the shirts should have no codal inference with whether he's going to default or not.
So because of that, we know that this feature may not be a valid feature.

aligned_pages.ppt_text:
Training Error and Generalization Error
• Training error (bias): model error on the training data
• Generalization error (variance): model error on new data
• Example: practice a future exam with past exams
• Doing well on past exams (training error) doesn’t guarantee a  good score on the future exam (generalization error)
• Student A gets 0 error on past exams by rote learning
• Student B  understands the reasons for givenanswers

Okay, so let's get started.
So before the break, we have mentioned that you should try to train a model with two layers and a model with six layers.
And one thing that you may have observed is that a six-layer model may not be as good as a simple model.
And this is weird because we have mentioned that deep neural network becomes powerful because we have more layers.
But now this is not the case.
So in the rest of this lecture, we are going to discuss several practical aspects in training neural network models.
So let's start with the easy thing.
Let's consider a practical scenario in our real life.

bullets.ai_comment: This topic introduces the real-world challenges that arise when training neural networks, motivated by a hands-on observation: a six-layer model may actually perform worse than a two-layer model, which seems counterintuitive given that deeper networks are theoretically more powerful. The teacher uses this surprising result as a launching point to explore why depth alone doesn't guarantee better performance. Understanding these practical aspects is critical because knowing the theory of deep learning is not enough — you must also know how to properly train and validate models to get good results in practice.

generated_pages.ppt_text: Predict Who Will Repay TheirLoans •A lender hires you to investigate who will repay their  loans •You are given complete files on 100 •5 defaulted within 3 years Image creditdebt.org

aligned_segments.text: That is, I may want to predict who will repay their loan and who will default.
So in such a scenario, a lender helps you to investigate this problem and you are given the files of 100 applicants.
Among these 100 applicants, there are five of them defaulted within three years.
So your goal is to build a model and predict who else is going to default.

bullets.ai_comment: This slide introduces a classic binary classification problem in machine learning: predicting loan repayment versus default. The teacher frames it as a real-world scenario where a lender needs an automated decision-support system. The goal is to build a predictive model that can identify which applicants are likely to default before granting them a loan. This type of problem is highly relevant in the financial industry for credit risk assessment.

bullets.ppt_text: A lender hires you to investigate who will repay their  loans
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher sets up the motivating scenario: you are brought in as a data scientist or analyst by a lending institution to solve a real business problem. The task is to investigate patterns in existing applicant data that distinguish repayers from defaulters. This framing emphasizes that machine learning models are built to serve practical decision-making needs, not just academic exercises. Understanding the business context is a critical first step before any modeling begins.

bullets.ppt_text: You are given complete files on 100
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher specifies that you have access to the complete records of 100 loan applicants, which serve as your training dataset. These files likely contain features such as income, credit history, employment status, and other financial details. Having 'complete files' implies rich, structured data that can be used to extract meaningful patterns. The relatively small dataset size (100 applicants) is notable and will likely be relevant to discussions about model generalization and overfitting.

Out of the 100 applicants, only 5 have actually defaulted within a three-year window, making this a heavily imbalanced classification problem (5% positive class). The teacher highlights this to show that the majority of cases are non-defaults, which poses a challenge: a naive model that always predicts 'repay' would already be 95% accurate but completely useless for identifying defaulters. This imbalance is a key concept in practical machine learning and motivates the need for appropriate evaluation metrics beyond simple accuracy.

generated_pages.ppt_text:
A Surprising Finding
• All 5 people who defaulted wore blue shirts during interviews
• Your model may find this strong signal as well

aligned_segments.text: For such a job, what you may find is they give you some photos and they find that all the five people who defaulted worn blue shirts during their interviews.
And your model also find that blue shirts is a strong signal for default.
So whether this is a valid feature or whether there are some problem when we make this conclusion.
This is a very classic problem of overfitting or there's no codal evidence that blue shirts actually predict applicants default behavior, right?
The reason is because all of them are wearing blue shirts.
So you cannot say only those who default wear such a blue shirts.
This is one thing.
And the other thing is using human logic, we know that the color of the shirts should have no codal inference with whether he's going to default or not.
So because of that, we know that this feature may not be a valid feature.

bullets.ai_comment: The teacher introduces a counterintuitive example to illustrate a fundamental machine learning pitfall. The 'surprising finding' refers to a model discovering a pattern that appears statistically strong but is actually meaningless. This sets up a discussion about the danger of blindly trusting what a model learns from data. It serves as a cautionary tale about the difference between correlation and causation in feature selection.

bullets.ppt_text: • All 5 people who defaulted wore blue shirts during interviews
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher describes a scenario where a dataset contains photos of loan applicants, and it turns out that all five people who defaulted happened to be wearing blue shirts during their interviews. This is a classic example of a spurious correlation — the sample size is extremely small (only 5 people), making any pattern found highly unreliable. The teacher emphasizes that with such a tiny group, you cannot generalize the observation to mean that blue shirts cause or predict default behavior, since non-defaulters may also have worn blue shirts.

bullets.ppt_text: • Your model may find this strong signal as well
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher warns that a machine learning model, lacking human common sense, will treat 'blue shirt' as a genuinely strong predictive feature if the training data shows a perfect correlation — even though this is statistically meaningless. This is a textbook case of overfitting, where the model latches onto noise or coincidence in the training data rather than learning a true underlying pattern. The teacher further highlights two problems: first, the absence of causal evidence linking shirt color to default behavior; and second, that human logic immediately tells us shirt color has no rational connection to financial behavior. This example underscores why domain knowledge and feature validation are critical steps before trusting any model output.

• Generalization error (variance): model error on new data

bullets.ai_comment: This slide introduces the foundational distinction between training error and generalization error, which is a classic and central topic in machine learning. The teacher signals this is an important conceptual discussion by calling it a 'classic' topic. Understanding this distinction is essential for diagnosing whether a model is overfitting or truly learning meaningful patterns. It sets the stage for understanding model evaluation and selection.

bullets.ppt_text: • Training error (bias): model error on the training data
bullets.ai_comment: Training error is defined as the error a model makes on the very data it was trained on. The teacher explains it straightforwardly: it measures how well the model fits the examples it has already seen. A very low training error might seem desirable, but it does not tell us how the model will behave on unseen data. This concept is also associated with bias in the bias-variance tradeoff framework.

bullets.ppt_text: • Generalization error (variance): model error on new data
bullets.ai_comment: Generalization error measures how well the model performs on data it has never seen before, which is the true goal of any machine learning system. The teacher emphasizes that this is the metric that actually matters in practice. It is linked to variance in the bias-variance framework, capturing how much the model's performance fluctuates across different datasets. A large gap between training error and generalization error is a warning sign of overfitting.

bullets.ppt_text: • Example: practice a future exam with past exams
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher uses the analogy of a student preparing for an upcoming exam by practicing with past exam papers. In this analogy, the past exams represent the training data and the future exam represents unseen, real-world data. This intuitive example makes the abstract concept immediately relatable to students. The teacher begins to introduce this analogy around the 04:56 mark, though the transcript is cut off mid-sentence.

bullets.ppt_text: • Doing well on past exams (training error) doesn't guarantee a  good score on the future exam (generalization error)
bullets.ai_comment: This is the core takeaway of the analogy: achieving a perfect or near-perfect score on past exams does not guarantee success on the actual future exam. The teacher reinforces this point at [04:47–04:56] by explicitly stating that 'the performance on the training data does not represent the performance on a new data set.' This is a critical insight because it explains why simply minimizing training error is an insufficient and potentially misleading objective in machine learning.

bullets.ppt_text: • Student A gets 0 error on past exams by rote learning
• Student B  understands the reasons for givenanswers
